title: Effectively Existentially-Atomic Structures
why_relevant: Directly studies structures whose automorphism orbits of tuples are definable by existential formulas, the core model-theoretic notion behind Sigma-in-1 definability. Its equivalences with Pi-2 Scott sentences and effective categoricity are likely central to proving or refuting the proposed separation.

title: A Robuster Scott Rank
why_relevant: Contains the key theorem equating Sigma-in-alpha definability of automorphism orbits, Pi-in-(alpha+1) Scott sentences, and uniform boldface Delta-alpha categoricity. The alpha = 1 case is especially relevant for relating existential orbit-definability to computable categoricity on a cone.

title: Complexity of Scott Sentences
why_relevant: Gives effective versions of results connecting Scott sentence complexity with definability of tuple orbits. Its results about finitely generated structures and definability of generating-tuple orbits are useful because countable automorphism groups often reduce automorphisms to finite bases.

title: Scott Complexity of Countable Structures
why_relevant: Classifies possible Scott complexities and constructs examples with delicate behavior after naming finitely many constants. These constructions and invariants are useful for building structures where finite parameters do not lower automorphism-orbit complexity to Sigma-in-1.

title: On Optimal Scott Sentences of Finitely Generated Algebraic Structures
why_relevant: Analyzes when finitely generated structures have optimal Sigma-3 or d-Sigma-2 Scott sentences in terms of definability of generating-tuple orbits. This is relevant if one tries to use a finite base for a countable automorphism group to define automorphisms from images of that base.

title: On the Computability of Optimal Scott Sentences
why_relevant: Separates low boldface Scott complexity from low computable Scott complexity. This is useful because the problem mixes computability on a cone with non-effective infinitary definability.

title: An Introduction to the Scott Complexity of Countable Structures and a Survey of Recent Results
why_relevant: A concise guide to Scott complexity, orbit-definability, and recent constructions. It is useful for locating normal-form arguments and known examples where finite parameters change, or fail to change, Scott complexity.

title: A Computability Theoretic Equivalent to Vaught's Conjecture
why_relevant: Develops the on-a-cone methodology and the hyperarithmetic-is-recursive phenomenon for countable structures. Its use of Sigma-in formulas, Scott rank, and cone arguments is closely analogous to computably AUT-countable on a cone.

title: Analytic Equivalence Relations Satisfying Hyperarithmetic-is-Recursive
why_relevant: Provides general cone theorems for analytic equivalence relations without perfectly many classes. The automorphism/copy setting can be viewed through analytic equivalence relations and closed Polish group actions, making this a likely source of proof techniques.

title: Degree Spectra of Relations on a Cone
why_relevant: Studies exactly how definability and intrinsic computability of relations behave on cones. The graph of an automorphism is a binary relation/function, so the paper’s cone-spectrum results are directly relevant to turning non-Sigma-in-1 definability into noncomputable copies or proving such a move impossible.

title: A Relation on (omega,<) of Intermediate Degree Spectrum on a Cone
why_relevant: Gives recent constructions showing that degree spectra of relations on a cone can have subtle intermediate behavior. This is relevant for testing whether non-existential definability of an automorphism graph must force noncomputable behavior on a cone.

title: Simple Structures with Complex Symmetry
why_relevant: Introduces and develops automorphism spectra of computable structures. Since the problem is about the computability of all automorphisms in all copies on a cone, its realization and non-realization results for automorphism degrees are directly relevant.

title: A Computable Functor From Graphs to Fields
why_relevant: Constructs computable functors preserving essential computable-model-theoretic properties, including automorphism spectra. This is useful for transferring any graph-based construction solving the problem into fields or other algebraic settings.

title: Computable Functors and Effective Interpretability
why_relevant: Equates computable functorial reductions with effective interpretations. These tools help transfer computability of automorphisms, definability of relations, and cone behavior between coded structures.

title: Borel Functors and Infinitary Interpretations
why_relevant: Connects Baire-measurable functors and homomorphisms of automorphism groups with infinitary interpretations, with preservation of complexity. This is useful for the descriptive-set-theoretic side of automorphism groups and representations.

title: Computability Theoretic Classifications for Classes of Structures
why_relevant: Surveys the standard toolbox for properties on a cone, Sigma-small classes, back-and-forth types, and effective bi-interpretability. It provides context and techniques for turning a cone hypothesis into structural definability information.

title: Punctual Definability on Structures
why_relevant: Characterizes punctual categoricity on a cone using existential atomicity, local finiteness, and finite automorphic triviality. Although the notion is different, its methods are closely related to proving that on-cone computability assumptions force finite-parameter existential definability.

title: Computable Polish Group Actions
why_relevant: Generalizes Montalban-style cone/categoricity theorems to computable Polish transformation groups. Since copies of a countable structure and their automorphisms are naturally handled via Polish group actions, this gives a descriptive framework for the problem.

title: Effectively Closed Subgroups of the Infinite Symmetric Group
why_relevant: Automorphism groups of countable structures are closed subgroups of the infinite symmetric group, and computable presentations make them effectively closed. Results about computable elements, presentations, and complexity of such subgroups are directly applicable to computably AUT-countable structures.
